<findings>

[observation] [warning] High coupling (0.69) — 3 imports target "remote-integration"
[observation] [info] Cohesion 0.31 reflects two largely independent schema files grouped by external consumers rather than internal structure; this is architecturally acceptable for a schema barrel but indicates the zone boundary is consumer-driven, not domain-driven.
[observation] [warning] Coupling 0.69 is high for a schema-only zone — schemas are being imported by many upstream modules, which is expected but means any schema shape change will have broad blast radius.
[observation] [info] notion.ts lives here while the adapter and mapping logic live in the notion-integration zone — consider collocating or at minimum documenting that these two zones together form the full Notion integration layer.
[pattern] [warning] The index.ts barrel in integration-schemas re-exports both Jira and Notion schemas, but the Notion adapter lives in a separate zone with no import from this barrel — the barrel does not serve its natural consumer, undermining its purpose as a unified schema surface
[relationship] [warning] integration-schemas (pure schema types) is consumed by remote-integration (3 imports) but has no import link to notion-integration despite both serving the same external service — schema and adapter are decoupled from each other while both couple to separate internal zones, a leaky abstraction at the service boundary
[anti-pattern] [warning] The index.ts barrel re-exports both jira.ts and notion.ts schemas but notion-integration (the natural consumer of notion.ts) has no import link to this barrel — the barrel's primary consumer for Notion schema types is some zone other than the adapter that interprets those schemas, inverting the expected schema→adapter data-flow
[suggestion] [info] Pre-emptively create a jira-integration zone directory (src/store/jira/) before any Jira adapter code is written. Establishing the zone boundary before the code exists prevents the organic fragmentation that produced the notion-integration vs integration-schemas split. This is a process recommendation, not a code change.
[suggestion] [warning] Zone "Integration Schemas" (integration-schemas) has critical risk (score: 0.69, cohesion: 0.31, coupling: 0.69) — requires refactoring before new feature development

</findings>

<insights>

- High coupling (0.69) — 3 imports target "remote-integration"
- Grouping Jira and Notion schemas under a shared index.ts barrel is a clean pattern — changes to either integration's schema have a single, obvious location.
- Cohesion of 0.31 is below ideal; the Jira and Notion schemas are peers with no internal cross-dependency, so the community algorithm grouped them purely by shared consumers rather than internal coupling — this is acceptable for a schema barrel but worth noting.
- Ensure the index.ts re-exports are kept minimal and explicit; schema barrels that grow unchecked tend to expose internal draft types and create hidden coupling for consumers.
- Coupling 0.69 is high for a schema-only zone — schemas are being imported by many upstream modules, which is expected but means any schema shape change will have broad blast radius.
- Cohesion 0.31 reflects two largely independent schema files grouped by external consumers rather than internal structure; this is architecturally acceptable for a schema barrel but indicates the zone boundary is consumer-driven, not domain-driven.
- notion.ts lives here while the adapter and mapping logic live in the notion-integration zone — consider collocating or at minimum documenting that these two zones together form the full Notion integration layer.
- integration-schemas is a pure-data zone (no logic, only shape declarations) while notion-integration is a behavioral zone (adapter + mapping logic) — this data/behavior split across zones is architecturally asymmetric and means the schema barrel is tightly coupled to the behavioral zone's consumers without being co-located with the code that interprets those schemas
- integration-schemas (pure schema types) is consumed by remote-integration (3 imports) but has no import link to notion-integration despite both serving the same external service — schema and adapter are decoupled from each other while both couple to separate internal zones, a leaky abstraction at the service boundary
- The index.ts barrel in integration-schemas re-exports both Jira and Notion schemas, but the Notion adapter lives in a separate zone with no import from this barrel — the barrel does not serve its natural consumer, undermining its purpose as a unified schema surface
- integration-schemas does not appear as a source in cross-zone import traffic despite index.ts existing specifically to be consumed — this absence suggests the barrel's consumers are within the same algorithmic zone or consume it through file-level imports that bypass zone boundaries, making the barrel's actual downstream reach unverifiable from zone-level data alone
- The index.ts barrel re-exports both jira.ts and notion.ts schemas but notion-integration (the natural consumer of notion.ts) has no import link to this barrel — the barrel's primary consumer for Notion schema types is some zone other than the adapter that interprets those schemas, inverting the expected schema→adapter data-flow
- jira.ts has no counterpart behavioral zone — unlike Notion, which has notion-integration to house adapter and mapping logic, Jira has schema types only with no adapter zone anywhere in the codebase. If Jira adapter code is added, it will have no natural home and will likely accrete into whichever existing zone is most convenient at the time, repeating the fragmentation pattern of the Notion integration.
- Pre-emptively create a jira-integration zone directory (src/store/jira/) before any Jira adapter code is written. Establishing the zone boundary before the code exists prevents the organic fragmentation that produced the notion-integration vs integration-schemas split. This is a process recommendation, not a code change.
- [call graph] 1 internal calls, 3 outgoing, 5 incoming (cohesion: 0.25, coupling: 0.75)

</insights>
